splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions must be finalized for calculating employer withdrawal liability under ERISA's multiemployer pension provisions. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after the measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the end of the prior plan year, allowing use of more current data in the withdrawal liability calculation.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by the measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability but potentially requiring use of older assumptions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The split affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year and raises questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the period of supervised release is tolled while the supervisee is a fugitive, so that time spent absconding does not count toward the completion of the supervised release term.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term continues to run regardless of the supervisee's fugitive status.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face stricter gatekeeping than Congress may have intended; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, adding an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners under § 2255, and they need only meet § 2255(h)'s separate requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in § 3553(f)(1). The split produces dramatically different sentencing outcomes, as defendants with identical criminal histories may face mandatory minimums in some circuits but qualify for safety-valve relief in others. At least six circuits and seventeen judges have weighed in with competing interpretations.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), treating 'and' as conjunctive.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity should take precedence over deference to the Sentencing Commission's interpretive commentary when the Guidelines are ambiguous in a criminal context. The Third Circuit has held that deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails. Loper Bright's overruling of Chevron has amplified uncertainty about this issue.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal statutes or Sentencing Guidelines, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions should receive deference even in criminal cases, and lenity does not override that deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson, or whether it impermissibly expands the unambiguous guideline text. The split directly affects career offender designations and resulting sentence lengths.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid and entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) and that inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as controlled substance offenses for career offender purposes.

positions.label: Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline
positions.summary: Holds that the plain text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that Application Note 1 cannot override the guideline text to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling circumstances, while others allow courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to address this but some circuits have questioned the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute covers a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' is limited to substances controlled under federal law or also includes substances controlled only under state law. Some circuits hold that state-only controlled substances qualify, while others require the substance to be federally controlled. The split affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State-only controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the substance is also controlled under federal law, as there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the underlying substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 becomes part of the bankruptcy estate. The Ninth Circuit holds that such appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor. The split has significant practical implications for debtors deciding whether to convert their bankruptcy cases.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a), means that post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A)'s purpose establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to good-faith converting debtors, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. §§ 2241-55) as their exclusive remedy when challenging conditions of confinement that affect the duration or fact of imprisonment, or whether civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens) remain available.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy for challenges that implicate the fact or duration of confinement, or whether certain conditions-of-confinement claims can proceed as civil rights actions. The Eighth Circuit has acknowledged this unresolved conflict spanning more than half a century, with circuits taking different approaches particularly in the context of BOP decisions about prerelease custody and earned time credits under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges the fact or duration of confinement, including BOP decisions affecting release timing, habeas corpus is the sole federal remedy and civil rights claims are barred.

positions.label: Civil rights actions may also be available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims challenging certain conditions or BOP decisions even when they implicate the fact or duration of confinement, particularly when habeas relief would be inadequate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate deadline applies to appeals from Hyde Amendment attorney's fee orders in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment — filed in a criminal case but seeking civil-type relief — is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but a minority of circuits apply the criminal deadline. The characterization affects whether notices of appeal are timely.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises out of and is sufficiently connected to the criminal case that the shorter criminal appeal deadline governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences at supervised release revocation that are expressly based on punishing the defendant for the violation, or whether revocation sentences must serve only the limited purposes of promoting respect for conditions and deterring future violations.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that the purposes of revocation sentencing are more limited and do not include retributive punishment for the violation conduct itself. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: Punishment for the violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for their violations of supervised release when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation sentences are more limited
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the defendant's breach of trust and the need to promote respect for conditions and deter future violations, not as retributive punishment for the violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine when a motion to seal such records is denied. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized medical privacy denials as qualifying for collateral order appeal, creating potential disagreement about the scope of the doctrine.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that the interest in medical privacy is of sufficiently high order that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment and thus qualifies for immediate appeal.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a sealing motion, even one involving medical information, is not the type of order qualifying for collateral order appeal because other remedies may adequately protect the interest.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' a broker's services, preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for their role in selecting or monitoring motor carriers. The split affects liability allocation in the trucking industry and access to state tort remedies for accident victims.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption provision bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently hiring or retaining motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are too attenuated from broker 'services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Aspen American Insurance Co. v. Landstar Ranger, Inc.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The outcome could reshape how courts evaluate candidate standing, potentially making it far easier or harder to challenge election administration practices before elections take place.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly dilute or affect the counting of votes, based on competitive injury or vote-dilution theories.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate does not have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election absent a concrete, particularized injury, as generalized complaints about voting procedures are insufficient.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Jacobson v. Florida Secretary of State

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may incorporate arguments about legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence — claims that might otherwise be raised in habeas proceedings under § 2255. Some circuits allow such considerations as part of the totality of circumstances, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not serve as an end-run around habeas limitations.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider legal errors or changes affecting the validity of a conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not circumvent habeas through compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas corpus, and claims about conviction errors must be raised under § 2255 rather than § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether the longstanding prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances survives Second Amendment scrutiny when applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute while others have found it unconstitutional as applied.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) is consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation and does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons who use intoxicating substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
